name: Hoori
description: Hoori, also known as Hikohohodemi no Mikoto, is a significant figure in Japanese mythology, particularly within the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki accounts of the imperial lineage. He is the youngest of two brothers, the other being Hoderi, and is often associated with the sea and fishing, contrasting with his brother's association with hunting. Hoori's story is a crucial element in legitimizing the divine ancestry of the Japanese imperial family, linking them to the gods and goddesses of the Shinto pantheon. His lineage traces back to Amaterasu, the sun goddess, emphasizing the celestial origins of the ruling dynasty. Hoori's interactions with the sea god Ryujin and his subsequent marriage to Ryujin's daughter, Toyotama-hime, are pivotal events that contribute to the imperial family's perceived authority and right to rule. He is revered as a symbol of prosperity, maritime prowess, and the divine connection between the land and the sea.
subtitle: The Fire Subduing Prince

keyMyths.title: The Lost Fishing Hook
keyMyths.description: Hoori borrowed his elder brother Hoderi's fishing hook, but unfortunately lost it in the sea. Hoderi demanded its return, leading to a conflict between the brothers. Hoori searched tirelessly but could not find the hook. Distraught, he sought help from the sea god, Ryujin, who offered him assistance. This myth highlights the importance of fulfilling obligations and the consequences of carelessness, while also demonstrating Hoori's perseverance and eventual good fortune.

keyMyths.title: Marriage to Toyotama-hime
keyMyths.description: Ryujin helped Hoori find the lost fishing hook and, furthermore, offered his daughter, Toyotama-hime, in marriage. Hoori and Toyotama-hime lived happily in the sea palace for several years. However, Hoori eventually longed to return to his homeland. Toyotama-hime agreed to accompany him, but she requested that he not watch her give birth. Curiosity got the better of Hoori, and he broke his promise, leading to Toyotama-hime's departure back to the sea.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Ugayafukiaezu
keyMyths.description: Toyotama-hime, ashamed that Hoori witnessed her birth in her true form (a dragon or shark), returned to the sea after giving birth to Ugayafukiaezu. She entrusted her sister, Tamayori-hime, to raise the child. Ugayafukiaezu later married Tamayori-hime, and they had a son named Jimmu, who is considered the first Emperor of Japan. This myth establishes the divine lineage of the imperial family and their connection to both the land and the sea.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Represents his connection to the sea and his skills as a fisherman.

associations.name: Fish
associations.significance: Symbolizes his domain and the central role of fishing in his mythology.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The moon is associated with water and the cyclical nature of life, reflecting Hoori's journey and eventual return.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Represents the ocean and the vastness of his underwater experiences.

associations.name: Reconciliation
associations.significance: His story emphasizes the resolution of conflict and the restoration of harmony between brothers and realms.

associations.name: Lineage
associations.significance: As an ancestor of the imperial line, he represents the divine origin and legitimacy of the Japanese emperors.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Seafood
worshipPractices.description: Due to his association with the sea, shrines dedicated to Hoori often receive offerings of fish and other seafood to ensure bountiful catches and safe voyages.

worshipPractices.name: Prayers for Safe Voyages
worshipPractices.description: Fishermen and sailors pray to Hoori for protection from storms and other dangers at sea, seeking his blessing for successful and safe journeys.

festivals.name: Fisherman's Festivals
festivals.timing: Various times throughout the year, depending on the region
festivals.description: Many coastal communities hold festivals honoring Hoori, celebrating the sea's bounty and praying for continued prosperity and safety for those who depend on it.

cultural.modernLegacy: Hoori's story continues to be referenced in Japanese popular culture, literature, and art, often symbolizing themes of reconciliation, perseverance, and the connection between humanity and nature. He is seen as a symbol of Japan's maritime heritage.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: Hoori, also known as Hikohohodemi no Mikoto, is the son of Ninigi-no-Mikoto, who was sent down from the heavens to pacify Japan. He is the youngest of two brothers, the elder being Hoderi. His birth is significant as it establishes a crucial link in the lineage that leads to the first Emperor of Japan, Emperor Jimmu. The circumstances surrounding his birth are not explicitly detailed in the Kojiki or Nihon Shoki, but his divine parentage underscores his importance as a figure destined for greatness. His very name, Hikohohodemi, suggests a radiant and fiery presence, hinting at his divine nature and the power he would wield. His role as a progenitor of the imperial line makes him a central figure in Japanese mythology and national identity.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Hoori's most famous myth involves the loss of his brother Hoderi's fishhook. After losing the hook in the sea, Hoori descends to the underwater palace of the Dragon King Ryūjin. There, he discovers the hook and marries the Dragon King's daughter, Toyotama-hime. He lives in the underwater palace for three years before longing for his homeland. Ryūjin helps him return, advising him on how to reclaim the lost hook and subdue his brother. Upon his return, using the magical jewels given to him by the Dragon King, Hoori manipulates the tides to assist his brother. Eventually, Hoderi submits to Hoori, and peace is restored. Toyotama-hime later follows Hoori to land to give birth to their son, Ugayafukiaezu, but she asks Hoori not to watch her during childbirth. He breaks this promise, and she transforms into a dragon before returning to the sea, leaving her son to be raised by Hoori's sister, Tamayori-hime.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Hoori's legacy is deeply intertwined with the Japanese imperial lineage. As the grandfather of Emperor Jimmu, the first emperor of Japan, Hoori's story serves as a foundational myth for the imperial family's divine origins and legitimacy. His tale is not only a mythological narrative but also a symbolic representation of the relationship between the land and the sea, as well as the importance of reconciliation and harmony. The motif of the lost fishhook has been interpreted in various ways, representing themes of loss, recovery, and the cyclical nature of life. Hoori's character continues to inspire artistic and literary works, reinforcing his enduring presence in Japanese culture. He embodies the connection between the divine and the earthly, and his story serves as a reminder of the values of perseverance, diplomacy, and respect for the natural world.

shortDescription: A deity from Japanese mythology associated with the sea. His tale of the lost fishhook connects him to the imperial lineage and themes of reconciliation.
